title: Quantum physics
summary: Quantum physics is where light and matter stop behaving like the tidy waves and particles of classical physics and start doing both at once. This Higher Level lesson builds the full E.2 toolkit: the photoelectric effect and why it forced physicists to accept the photon; the work function, threshold frequency and Einstein's photoelectric equation; the electronvolt and the stopping-potential experiment; photon energy from frequency and wavelength; wave–particle duality; the de Broglie wavelength of matter and the electron-diffraction evidence for it; and, qualitatively, Heisenberg's uncertainty principle. Every worked example is laid out exactly the way our marking engine awards marks, because in a calculation topic how you show your working decides how many marks you keep.
learningObjectives: Describe the photoelectric effect and explain why its features are evidence for the photon model of light rather than the classical wave model.
Define the work function and threshold frequency, and apply Einstein's photoelectric equation $E_{max}=hf-\phi$ using the electronvolt and the stopping potential.
Calculate photon energy from $E=hf=hc/\lambda$ and the de Broglie wavelength of matter from $\lambda = h/p$.
Explain how electron diffraction provides evidence for the wave nature of matter, and state the Heisenberg uncertainty principle qualitatively.
Present working so that method marks and error-carried-forward (ECF) protect the final answer.

simpleExplanation.title: Light that Behaves Like Bullets
simpleExplanation.summary: Shine light on a metal and, under the right conditions, electrons are ejected. Classical physics — light as a continuous wave — predicts the wrong behaviour completely. The results only make sense if light arrives in discrete packets of energy called photons, each delivering its energy to a single electron all at once. The same duality runs backwards too: electrons, which we think of as particles, can diffract like waves.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine trying to knock a coconut off a shelf. A classical 'wave' is like a gentle, continuous breeze — blow long enough and hard enough and surely the coconut must eventually fall. But the photoelectric effect says otherwise: it is like throwing balls. One ball below a certain size will NEVER dislodge the coconut, no matter how many you throw per second. Only a single ball above the critical size does the job — and any extra size becomes the coconut's leftover speed. Each photon is one such ball, and it must act alone.
steps: Treat light as a stream of photons, each carrying energy $E=hf$ set only by the frequency, not the brightness.
For a photon to eject an electron, its energy must at least equal the work function $\phi$ — the minimum energy binding the electron to the metal.
Any energy the photon carries above $\phi$ becomes the electron's maximum kinetic energy: $E_{max}=hf-\phi$.
Turn the argument around: since waves carry momentum, a particle with momentum $p$ has a wavelength $\lambda = h/p$, and firing electrons at a crystal produces a diffraction pattern that proves it.

flashcards.back: The emission of electrons (photoelectrons) from a metal surface when electromagnetic radiation of high enough frequency is shone on it. Its features cannot be explained by the classical wave model of light.
Light is quantised into packets called photons, each of energy $E=hf=hc/\lambda$, where $h=6.63\times10^{-34}$ J s. The energy depends only on frequency (or wavelength), not on intensity.
The minimum energy needed to remove an electron from the surface of a particular metal. It is a property of the metal only.
The minimum frequency of light that can eject an electron from a given metal, given by $\phi = hf_0$. Below $f_0$, no electrons are emitted no matter how intense the light or how long it shines.
$E_{max}=hf-\phi$. One photon delivers energy $hf$ to one electron; $\phi$ is spent escaping the metal and the remainder is the electron's maximum kinetic energy.
The energy gained by an electron accelerated through a potential difference of 1 V: $1\text{ eV}=1.60\times10^{-19}$ J. A convenient energy unit at the quantum scale.
The reverse voltage that just stops the most energetic photoelectrons, so $E_{max}=eV_s$. Measuring $V_s$ gives $E_{max}$ directly, and it does not depend on light intensity.

Classically, brighter light (more intensity) should give more energetic electrons. Experiment shows intensity only changes the NUMBER of electrons per second; their maximum kinetic energy depends solely on frequency — exactly what the photon model predicts.

Both light and matter exhibit wave properties (interference, diffraction) and particle properties (photons, discrete collisions). Which behaviour appears depends on the experiment; neither picture alone is complete.
Any particle of momentum $p=mv$ has an associated wavelength $\lambda = h/p$. Larger momentum means a shorter wavelength, which is why everyday objects show no observable wave behaviour.

Firing electrons through a thin crystal or graphite produces concentric diffraction rings — a wave phenomenon. It is direct experimental evidence that matter has a wavelength, confirming $\lambda = h/p$.

It is impossible to know both the position and the momentum of a particle with unlimited precision at the same time: $\Delta x \, \Delta p \gtrsim \dfrac{h}{4\pi}$. Pinning down one makes the other more uncertain. It is a fundamental limit, not a measurement flaw.

No. Doubling the intensity of light of the same frequency doubles the RATE of photoelectron emission but leaves the maximum kinetic energy (and stopping potential) unchanged. Only frequency changes $E_{max}$.
In the photoelectric effect, one photon interacts with one electron. An electron cannot 'save up' energy from several below-threshold photons — that is why $f_0$ is a hard cut-off.

quickCheck.prompt: In the photoelectric effect, the interaction between light and electrons is best described as:
options: One photon shares its energy among many electrons
Many photons combine their energy to free one electron
One photon delivers its energy to one electron
Electrons absorb energy gradually from the light wave
quickCheck.answer: One photon delivers its energy to one electron

quickCheck.prompt: Light of frequency below the threshold frequency $f_0$ is shone very intensely on a metal for a long time. What happens?
options: Electrons are emitted, but slowly
No electrons are ever emitted, regardless of intensity or time
Electrons are emitted once enough energy has built up
Electrons are emitted only if the light is left on long enough
quickCheck.answer: No electrons are ever emitted, regardless of intensity or time

quickCheck.prompt: The de Broglie wavelength of a particle of momentum $p$ is given by:
options: $\lambda = hp$
$\lambda = h/p$
$\lambda = p/h$
$\lambda = hf$

quickCheck.prompt: The energy of a single photon of light of frequency $f$ is:
options: $E = hf$
$E = h/f$
$E = f/h$

quickCheck.prompt: Doubling the intensity of light (same frequency) above the threshold frequency will:
options: Double the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons
Double the number of photoelectrons emitted per second
Double the stopping potential
Have no effect on the number of photoelectrons
quickCheck.answer: Double the number of photoelectrons emitted per second

faq.q: Why did the photoelectric effect force physicists to abandon the wave model of light?
faq.a: The classical wave model makes three predictions that all fail. It says a bright enough light of ANY frequency should eventually free electrons — but below the threshold frequency none ever appear. It says brighter light should give MORE energetic electrons — but intensity changes only the number, not the energy. And it predicts a time delay while energy accumulates — but emission is instantaneous. Einstein's photon model, where one quantum of energy $hf$ is delivered to one electron at once, explains all three perfectly.

faq.q: What is the difference between the work function and the threshold frequency?
faq.a: They describe the same thing from two angles. The work function $\phi$ is the minimum ENERGY needed to free an electron; the threshold frequency $f_0$ is the minimum FREQUENCY of photon that supplies that energy. They are linked by $\phi = hf_0$. Both are properties of the metal alone.

faq.q: How does the stopping potential let us measure the electrons' energy?
faq.a: You apply a reverse voltage that pushes emitted electrons back toward the metal. Increase it until even the fastest electrons are just turned back and the photocurrent falls to zero — that voltage is the stopping potential $V_s$. Because the work done against the field then equals the electrons' maximum kinetic energy, $E_{max}=eV_s$, so measuring a voltage gives you an energy directly.

faq.q: If electrons are waves, why don't we notice matter waves in everyday life?
faq.a: Because the de Broglie wavelength $\lambda = h/p$ is fantastically small for macroscopic objects. Planck's constant is about $6.6\times10^{-34}$ J s, so anything with everyday momentum has a wavelength trillions of times smaller than an atom — far too small to produce any observable diffraction. Only for tiny, slow particles like electrons does the wavelength grow large enough to matter.

faq.q: Is the uncertainty principle just about clumsy measuring instruments?
faq.a: No — it is a fundamental property of nature, not a limitation of our equipment. Even with a perfect instrument, a particle simply does not possess a precise position and a precise momentum at the same time. The more sharply one is defined, the more the other is spread out, as expressed by $\Delta x \, \Delta p \gtrsim h/4\pi$.

sections.content: By 1900, light was firmly understood as a wave — Young's double slit and Maxwell's equations had settled the matter. Then a handful of stubborn experiments refused to fit, and within three decades physics had to accept that light comes in particles and that particles travel as waves. This Higher Level lesson follows that revolution through the photoelectric effect, the photon, the de Broglie wavelength and the uncertainty principle. As in every calculation topic, the worked examples are laid out exactly the way our engine marks your own practice: the method is on the page, mark by mark.
The photoelectric effect: evidence for the photon

Shine ultraviolet light on a clean metal plate and electrons are ejected from its surface. These ejected electrons are called photoelectrons, and the phenomenon is the photoelectric effect. What made it revolutionary was not that electrons come off, but the precise WAY they come off — a way the wave model of light gets completely wrong.

items: **Existence of a threshold frequency.** Below a certain frequency $f_0$, no electrons are emitted at all — no matter how intense the light or how long it shines. The wave model predicts that any frequency should work if the light is bright enough.
**Instantaneous emission.** Above $f_0$, electrons appear the instant the light arrives, with no time delay for energy to 'build up'. The wave model predicts a measurable lag.

**Maximum kinetic energy depends on frequency, not intensity.** Increasing the frequency increases the electrons' maximum kinetic energy; increasing the intensity does not. The wave model predicts brighter light should give more energetic electrons.
**Intensity controls the number, not the energy.** Brighter light of the same frequency simply ejects more electrons per second.

sections.content: Einstein resolved every one of these puzzles with a single bold idea: light itself is quantised into packets — photons — each carrying an energy fixed by the frequency. A photon is absorbed by one electron in an all-or-nothing event. This is why a below-threshold photon can never free an electron even in a bright beam: each electron only ever meets one photon at a time, and if that single photon is too weak, nothing happens.
Photon energy, the work function and Einstein's equation
The energy of a single photon depends only on the frequency $f$ of the light (or equivalently its wavelength $\lambda$, since $c = f\lambda$).
E = hf = \frac{hc}{\lambda}

To escape the metal, an electron must be supplied with at least a minimum amount of energy called the work function, $\phi$ — a property of the metal alone. The lowest-frequency photon that can just supply this energy defines the threshold frequency, through $\phi = hf_0$. When a photon of higher frequency arrives, it spends $\phi$ freeing the electron and hands the leftover energy to the electron as kinetic energy. The most weakly bound electrons keep the most, so the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons is Einstein's photoelectric equation:
E_{max} = hf - \phi

items: $hf$ is the energy delivered by one photon.
$\phi = hf_0$ is the work function — the 'entry fee' to leave the metal.
$E_{max}$ is the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons. If $hf < \phi$, then $E_{max}$ would be negative, which is impossible — so no emission occurs.
A graph of $E_{max}$ against $f$ is a straight line of gradient $h$, with $x$-intercept $f_0$ and $y$-intercept $-\phi$.

sections.content: The electronvolt and the stopping potential

At the quantum scale, the joule is an awkwardly large unit, so we use the electronvolt (eV): the energy an electron gains when accelerated through a potential difference of 1 volt. Since energy transferred is charge times voltage, $1\text{ eV} = e \times 1\text{ V} = 1.60\times10^{-19}$ J. To convert an energy from joules to electronvolts, divide by $1.60\times10^{-19}$; to go the other way, multiply.

How do we actually measure $E_{max}$? By making the electrons pay it back. In the experiment, a reverse potential difference is applied that pushes emitted electrons back toward the metal. As this voltage is increased, fewer electrons have enough energy to cross, until at the stopping potential $V_s$ even the fastest electron is just turned back and the current falls to zero. The work done against the field, $eV_s$, then equals the maximum kinetic energy:
E_{max} = eV_s

The stopping potential depends only on the frequency of the light and the metal — never on the intensity. If an exam question doubles the brightness and asks what happens to the stopping potential, the answer is 'no change'. More intensity means more electrons per second, but each of the fastest ones has exactly the same maximum kinetic energy.

sections.question: Light of frequency 8.0×10¹⁴ Hz strikes a metal of work function 2.0 eV. Calculate the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons in eV. (h = 6.63×10⁻³⁴ J s; 1 eV = 1.6×10⁻¹⁹ J) [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer — full working.**

**Step 1 — Photon energy in joules.** Use $E = hf$.
$E = (6.63\times10^{-34})\times(8.0\times10^{14}) = 5.30\times10^{-19}$ J.

**Step 2 — Convert the photon energy to electronvolts** so it can be compared with the work function, which is given in eV.
$E = \dfrac{5.30\times10^{-19}}{1.6\times10^{-19}} = 3.31$ eV.

**Step 3 — Apply Einstein's photoelectric equation** $E_{max}=hf-\phi$, working in eV throughout.
$E_{max} = 3.31 - 2.0 = 1.31$ eV.

**Final answer:** the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons is $E_{max} \approx 1.3$ eV.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks:**

- **M1 — method for photon energy.** Awarded for using $E = hf$ with the correct values, $E = (6.63\times10^{-34})(8.0\times10^{14})$. This is a METHOD mark: it is earned for the correct relationship even if the arithmetic later slips.
- **A1 — photon energy in eV.** Awarded for $E = 5.30\times10^{-19}$ J $= 3.31$ eV. The engine accepts the joule value and its correct conversion to electronvolts.
- **M1 — apply $E_{max}=hf-\phi$.** Awarded for correctly subtracting the work function from the photon energy in consistent units: $E_{max} = 3.31 - 2.0$. Mixing units here (subtracting 2.0 eV from a value left in joules) loses this mark.
- **A1 — final answer.** Awarded for $E_{max} = 1.31$ eV (accept 1.3 eV). ECF protects you: a student who mis-keyed the photon energy but then correctly did (their $hf$) $-$ 2.0 still earns this mark on their own figure.

**'Accept answers that round correctly.'** The engine accepts 1.3 eV or 1.31 eV — it does not demand a fixed number of decimal places, only a correctly-rounded final value with the right unit.

**Bottom line:** two of the four marks are method marks that survive an arithmetic slip, and the answer marks are shielded by ECF — but only if the working is shown. A student who writes just '1.31 eV' with no steps risks losing 2–3 marks if the number is wrong.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR working exactly this way — analytically, mark by mark, with ECF. So always show the method: write $E = hf$, do the conversion to eV explicitly, then apply $E_{max}=hf-\phi$ in consistent units before quoting the final answer. The method marks and error-carried-forward are what protect your score when the final number slips, and you only get that protection if the working is on the page.

sections.question: A metal has a threshold frequency of 5.5×10¹⁴ Hz. (a) Calculate the work function of the metal in joules. (b) Light of wavelength 4.0×10⁻⁷ m is shone on the metal. Determine the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons in joules. (h = 6.63×10⁻³⁴ J s; c = 3.0×10⁸ m s⁻¹.) [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer — full working.**

**(a) Work function** from $\phi = hf_0$.
$\phi = (6.63\times10^{-34})\times(5.5\times10^{14}) = 3.65\times10^{-19}$ J.

**(b) Photon energy** from $E = hc/\lambda$.
$E = \dfrac{(6.63\times10^{-34})(3.0\times10^{8})}{4.0\times10^{-7}} = 4.97\times10^{-19}$ J.

**Apply Einstein's equation** $E_{max}=hf-\phi = E - \phi$.
$E_{max} = 4.97\times10^{-19} - 3.65\times10^{-19} = 1.3\times10^{-19}$ J.

**Check:** the photon energy ($4.97\times10^{-19}$ J) exceeds the work function ($3.65\times10^{-19}$ J), so emission does occur, as expected since the incident light's frequency is above $f_0$.

**Final answer:** $\phi \approx 3.6\times10^{-19}$ J; $E_{max} \approx 1.3\times10^{-19}$ J.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks:**

- **M1 — work function method.** Awarded for $\phi = hf_0$ with correct values.
- **A1 — work function value.** Awarded for $\phi = 3.65\times10^{-19}$ J (accept $3.6\times10^{-19}$ J).
- **M1 — photon energy and photoelectric equation.** Awarded for using $E = hc/\lambda$ AND subtracting the work function, $E_{max} = E - \phi$, in consistent units.
- **A1 — final answer with unit.** Awarded for $E_{max} \approx 1.3\times10^{-19}$ J. ECF applies: a wrong $\phi$ or $E$ that is then subtracted correctly still earns this mark on the student's own figures.

sections.content: Wave–particle duality and the de Broglie wavelength

The photoelectric effect shows light behaving as particles, yet interference and diffraction show it behaving as a wave. Light is neither purely one nor the other — it has a dual nature, and which face it shows depends on the experiment. In 1924 Louis de Broglie made the daring proposal that this duality is symmetric: if waves can act like particles, then particles should act like waves. He assigned every particle of momentum $p = mv$ a wavelength:
\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{mv}

items: The larger a particle's momentum, the shorter its de Broglie wavelength.
Everyday objects have enormous momentum compared with $h$, so their wavelengths are unimaginably small and no wave behaviour is ever observed.
For an electron, which has a tiny mass, the wavelength can be comparable to atomic spacings — small enough to be seen in diffraction.
An electron accelerated through a potential difference $V$ gains kinetic energy $eV = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$, from which its momentum, and hence $\lambda$, can be found.

sections.content: Electron diffraction: evidence for matter waves

De Broglie's idea would have stayed a curiosity without proof — and the proof is electron diffraction. When a beam of electrons is fired through a thin layer of graphite or a crystal, it does not simply make a single bright spot as particles would. Instead it produces concentric rings on a screen, exactly the diffraction pattern a wave makes when it passes through a regularly spaced lattice. Diffraction is a purely wave phenomenon, so the pattern is direct evidence that the electrons travel as waves with a definite wavelength. Measuring the ring spacing gives a wavelength that agrees precisely with $\lambda = h/p$, confirming de Broglie's relation.

In 'evidence for' questions, name the phenomenon AND the property it demonstrates. Say that electron DIFFRACTION (or the ring pattern) is the evidence, and that diffraction is a WAVE behaviour, so it shows electrons — normally treated as particles — have a wavelength. A bare 'electrons diffract' without linking diffraction to wave behaviour rarely earns full marks.

sections.question: An electron travels at 2.0×10⁶ m s⁻¹. Calculate its de Broglie wavelength. (Mass of electron m = 9.11×10⁻³¹ kg; h = 6.63×10⁻³⁴ J s.) [3]
sections.solution:
**Model answer — full working.**

**Step 1 — Find the momentum** of the electron, $p = mv$.
$p = (9.11\times10^{-31})\times(2.0\times10^{6}) = 1.82\times10^{-24}$ kg m s⁻¹.

**Step 2 — Apply the de Broglie relation** $\lambda = h/p$.
$\lambda = \dfrac{6.63\times10^{-34}}{1.82\times10^{-24}}$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**
$\lambda = 3.6\times10^{-10}$ m.

**Final answer:** the electron's de Broglie wavelength is $\lambda \approx 3.6\times10^{-10}$ m — comparable to atomic spacings, which is why electrons diffract through crystals.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 3 marks:**

- **M1 — momentum.** Awarded for $p = mv$ with correct values, $p = (9.11\times10^{-31})(2.0\times10^{6})$.
- **M1 — de Broglie substitution.** Awarded for $\lambda = h/p$ with the momentum substituted correctly. ECF applies: if $p$ was mis-calculated but then divided into $h$ correctly, this mark still stands.
- **A1 — final answer with unit.** Awarded for $\lambda \approx 3.6\times10^{-10}$ m (accept $3.6\times10^{-10}$ to $3.64\times10^{-10}$ m).

sections.content: The Heisenberg uncertainty principle (qualitative)

If a particle is also a wave, it cannot be pinned to a single exact point — a wave is spread out. This intuition is captured by Werner Heisenberg's uncertainty principle: it is impossible to know both the position and the momentum of a particle with perfect precision at the same instant. The more precisely you fix one, the less precisely the other can be known. Written qualitatively, the product of the uncertainty in position $\Delta x$ and the uncertainty in momentum $\Delta p$ has a lower bound:
\Delta x \, \Delta p \gtrsim \frac{h}{4\pi}

items: This is a fundamental feature of nature, not a shortcoming of measuring instruments — even a perfect instrument cannot beat it.
Confine a particle to a very small region (small $\Delta x$) and its momentum becomes correspondingly uncertain (large $\Delta p$), and vice versa.
There is a matching uncertainty relation between energy and time, $\Delta E \, \Delta t \gtrsim \dfrac{h}{4\pi}$.
The effect is negligible for large objects because $h$ is so small, but it dominates behaviour at the atomic scale.

sections.content: Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Thinking intensity changes the electrons' energy** — brighter light of the same frequency ejects MORE electrons per second, but the maximum kinetic energy and the stopping potential are unchanged. Only frequency changes $E_{max}$.

**Believing below-threshold light works if it is bright or shines long enough** — it never does. One electron meets one photon, and a below-threshold photon simply lacks the energy $\phi$; electrons cannot pool energy from several photons.

**Mixing joules and electronvolts inside $E_{max}=hf-\phi$** — convert everything to the same unit before subtracting. Subtracting a work function in eV from a photon energy left in joules is a classic mark-losing slip.
**Confusing the work function $\phi$ with the threshold frequency $f_0$** — they are linked by $\phi = hf_0$ but $\phi$ is an energy and $f_0$ is a frequency. Do not equate their numerical values.
**Inverting the de Broglie relation** — it is $\lambda = h/p$, so LARGER momentum gives a SHORTER wavelength. Writing $\lambda = hp$ or $\lambda = p/h$ is wrong.
**Saying 'electrons diffract' without linking diffraction to wave behaviour** — the evidence argument needs the step that diffraction is a wave property, so the electrons must have a wavelength.
**Treating the uncertainty principle as an instrument limitation** — it is a fundamental limit; the particle does not simultaneously possess an exact position and an exact momentum.
**Dropping units or over-rounding mid-calculation** — carry extra figures through the working and round only the final answer; always attach the correct unit (J, eV, m, or Hz).

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

In Paper 2 the marks are analytic: each is tied to a specific line of working — a method mark (M) or an answer mark (A) — and error-carried-forward (ECF) means a wrong number early on does not have to cost you the marks that follow. But that protection only exists if your method is written down. The photoelectric worked example above shows this in full: $E=hf$ earns M1, the photon energy converted to eV earns A1, applying $E_{max}=hf-\phi$ earns a second M1, and the final $1.31$ eV earns the last A1 — with ECF shielding the answer marks if an earlier number slips. Study how each mark is earned by a specific line, and write your own solutions the same way.
Where this leads

The photon and the matter wave are the doorway to the rest of quantum physics. Photon energy $E=hf$ reappears in atomic spectra and the emission and absorption of light by energy levels; the de Broglie wavelength underpins why atoms have quantised orbits and why electrons behave as standing waves; and the uncertainty principle sets the ground rules for the full quantum-mechanical description of confined particles. Master the habit here — identify whether the wave or the particle picture applies, keep your energy units consistent, show every line — and the quantum topics that follow become variations on a method you already own.

sections.label: Get a Paper 2 calculation marked: solve a photoelectric problem with full working

subtopics.title: The quantum nature of matter
subtopics.detail: De Broglie hypothesis, electron diffraction, and the uncertainty principle for position-momentum. $ \Delta x \Delta p \ge h/(4\pi) $

subtopics.title: The wave function
subtopics.detail: The probabilistic interpretation of the wave function $\Psi$ and the role of measurement. Probability density is $|\Psi|^2$.

subtopics.title: The quantum nature of the atom
subtopics.detail: Energy levels, atomic transitions, and the Schrödinger model for the hydrogen atom. $ E = -13.6/n^2 \text{ eV} $

questionBank.prompt:
An electron is accelerated from rest through a potential difference of 150 V.
a) Calculate the final speed of the electron. [2]
b) Determine the de Broglie wavelength of the electron. [2]

markScheme.text: (M1) Equates kinetic energy to electric potential energy: $E_k = qV$ or $\frac{1}{2} m v^2 = qV$.
(A1) Correctly calculates speed: $v = \sqrt{2qV/m} = \sqrt{2 \times 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \times 150 / 9.11 \times 10^{-31}} = 7.26 \times 10^6 \text{ m s}^{-1}$.
(M1) Uses de Broglie wavelength formula: $\lambda = h/p$ or $\lambda = h/(mv)$.
(A1) Correctly calculates wavelength: $\lambda = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} / (9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 7.26 \times 10^6) = 1.00 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}$. (Allow 0.100 nm)

questionBank.modelAnswer:
a) The kinetic energy gained by the electron is equal to the work done on it by the electric field, $E_k = qV$. So, $\frac{1}{2} m_e v^2 = qV$.
Rearranging for speed $v$:
$v = \sqrt{\frac{2qV}{m_e}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times (1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}) \times (150 \text{ V})}{9.11 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}}}$
$v \approx 7.26 \times 10^6 \text{ m s}^{-1}$.

b) The de Broglie wavelength is given by $\lambda = \frac{h}{p}$, where $p = m_e v$.
$\lambda = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}}{(9.11 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}) \times (7.26 \times 10^6 \text{ m s}^{-1})}$
$\lambda \approx 1.00 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}$.

questionBank.prompt:
The one-dimensional wave function $\Psi(x)$ for an electron confined in a box of length $L$ is shown. The electron is in its ground state ($n=1$).

(A diagram shows a single sinusoidal arch, starting at 0 at x=0, peaking at x=L/2, and returning to 0 at x=L. The vertical axis is labelled $\Psi(x)$.)

a) State what is meant by the wave function of an electron. [1]
b) The probability of finding the electron in a small region $\Delta x$ around a point $x$ is proportional to $|\Psi(x)|^2 \Delta x$. Outline where the electron is most likely to be found and where it is least likely to be found. [3]
c) The box has a length $L = 0.20$ nm. Determine the momentum of the electron. [2]

markScheme.text: (A1) The wave function is a (complex) function whose modulus squared gives the probability density of finding the particle at a particular point.
(M1) Most likely where $|\Psi(x)|^2$ is maximum / where $\Psi(x)$ has maximum amplitude.
(A1) This occurs at the centre of the box, $x = L/2$.
(A1) Least likely where $|\Psi(x)|^2$ is zero, which is at the edges of the box ($x=0$ and $x=L$).
(M1) For the ground state in a box, $L = \lambda/2$. The momentum is $p = h/\lambda = h/(2L)$.

questionBank.modelAnswer:
a) The wave function $\Psi$ is a mathematical function that contains all the information about a quantum system. The square of its modulus, $|\Psi|^2$, represents the probability density of finding the particle at a specific position and time.

b) The probability of finding the electron is proportional to $|\Psi(x)|^2$.
- The electron is most likely to be found where $|\Psi(x)|$ is a maximum. From the graph, this is in the centre of the box at $x = L/2$.
- The electron is least likely to be found where $|\Psi(x)|$ is zero. This occurs at the boundaries of the box, at $x=0$ and $x=L$.

c) For the ground state ($n=1$) of a particle in a box, the length of the box contains half a de Broglie wavelength, so $L = \frac{\lambda}{2}$.
The de Broglie wavelength is therefore $\lambda = 2L = 2 \times 0.20 \text{ nm} = 0.40 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$.
The momentum is given by the de Broglie relation $p = \frac{h}{\lambda}$.
$p = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}}{0.40 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}} \approx 1.7 \times 10^{-24} \text{ kg m s}^{-1}$.

questionBank.prompt:
The energy levels for a hydrogen atom are given by the formula $E_n = -\frac{13.6}{n^2}$ eV, where $n$ is the principal quantum number.
a) Calculate the energy, in joules, of a photon emitted when an electron transitions from the $n=3$ state to the $n=2$ state. [3]
b) This transition is part of the Balmer series. Explain what is meant by an emission spectrum and why it consists of discrete lines. [2]

markScheme.text: (M1) Calculates energy of levels: $E_3 = -13.6/3^2 = -1.51$ eV and $E_2 = -13.6/2^2 = -3.40$ eV.
(M1) Calculates energy difference: $\Delta E = E_3 - E_2 = -1.51 - (-3.40) = 1.89$ eV.
(A1) Converts energy to joules: $\Delta E = 1.89 \text{ eV} \times 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J/eV} = 3.02 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$.
(A1) An emission spectrum is the set of frequencies/wavelengths of EM radiation emitted by excited atoms; it appears as bright lines on a dark background.

(A1) Electrons in atoms can only exist in discrete/quantized energy levels. When they transition to a lower level, they emit a photon with energy equal to the exact energy difference, leading to discrete spectral lines.

questionBank.modelAnswer:
a) First, we calculate the energy of the initial and final states in eV.
$E_3 = -\frac{13.6}{3^2} = -\frac{13.6}{9} \approx -1.511 \text{ eV}$
$E_2 = -\frac{13.6}{2^2} = -\frac{13.6}{4} = -3.40 \text{ eV}$
The energy of the emitted photon is the difference between these levels:
$\Delta E = E_{\text{initial}} - E_{\text{final}} = E_3 - E_2 = (-1.511) - (-3.40) = 1.889 \text{ eV}$
To convert this to joules, we multiply by the elementary charge:
$\Delta E = 1.889 \text{ eV} \times (1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J/eV}) \approx 3.02 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$.

b) An emission spectrum is the spectrum of light emitted by a substance when its atoms are excited. It appears as a series of bright lines on a dark background.
The lines are discrete because, according to quantum mechanics, electrons in an atom can only occupy specific, quantized energy levels. When an electron de-excites from a higher energy level to a lower one, it emits a photon. The energy of this photon is precisely equal to the energy difference between the two levels ($E_{\text{photon}} = \Delta E_{\text{atom}}$). Since only discrete energy differences are possible, only photons of specific, discrete energies (and therefore specific frequencies/wavelengths) can be emitted, resulting in a line spectrum.

questionBank.prompt:
An experiment is designed to measure the position of an electron, which is known to be moving with a speed of $(5.00 \pm 0.01) \times 10^5 \text{ m s}^{-1}$.
a) Calculate the minimum uncertainty in the position of the electron along its direction of motion. [3]
b) The electron is part of a beam that is passed through a single slit of width $a$. The first minimum of the diffraction pattern is observed at an angle $\theta$. It is suggested that the uncertainty in the electron's position perpendicular to its motion can be approximated by the slit width $a$, and the uncertainty in its momentum in that same direction can be approximated by the momentum component associated with the angle $\theta$. Using the single-slit diffraction formula $\sin\theta \approx \lambda/a$ for the first minimum and the de Broglie hypothesis, deduce the Heisenberg uncertainty principle in the form $\Delta x \Delta p_y \approx h$. [3]

markScheme.text: (M1) Calculates uncertainty in momentum: $\Delta p = m \Delta v = (9.11 \times 10^{-31}) \times (2 \times 0.01 \times 10^5) = 1.822 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg m s}^{-1}$. (Must use the full range of uncertainty for $\Delta v$).
(M1) Uses Heisenberg uncertainty principle: $\Delta x \Delta p \ge h/(4\pi)$.
(A1) Calculates minimum uncertainty in position: $\Delta x_{\text{min}} = h/(4\pi \Delta p) = (6.63 \times 10^{-34}) / (4\pi \times 1.822 \times 10^{-27}) = 2.90 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}$.
(M1) Identifies uncertainty in position as slit width: $\Delta x \approx a$. And uncertainty in transverse momentum as $\Delta p_y \approx p \sin\theta$.
(M1) Combines the relations: $\Delta x \Delta p_y \approx a \times (p \sin\theta)$.
(A1) Uses $p=h/\lambda$ and $\sin\theta \approx \lambda/a$ to show $\Delta x \Delta p_y \approx a \times ( (h/\lambda) \times (\lambda/a) ) = h$.

questionBank.modelAnswer:
a) The uncertainty in the speed is the full range of possible values, $\Delta v = (5.01 - 4.99) \times 10^5 = 0.02 \times 10^5 \text{ m s}^{-1}$.
The uncertainty in the momentum is $\Delta p = m_e \Delta v$.
$\Delta p = (9.11 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}) \times (0.02 \times 10^5 \text{ m s}^{-1}) = 1.822 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg m s}^{-1}$.
The Heisenberg uncertainty principle states $\Delta x \Delta p \ge \frac{h}{4\pi}$.
The minimum uncertainty in position is therefore:
$\Delta x_{\text{min}} = \frac{h}{4\pi \Delta p} = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}}{4\pi \times (1.822 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg m s}^{-1})} \approx 2.90 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}$.

b) When an electron passes through a slit of width $a$, its position in the direction perpendicular to motion (y-direction) is known to within the width of the slit. We approximate the uncertainty in position as $\Delta x \approx a$.
Due to diffraction, the electron acquires a momentum component in the y-direction. The first minimum occurs at an angle $\theta$ where $\sin\theta = \lambda/a$. We approximate the uncertainty in the transverse momentum, $\Delta p_y$, by the magnitude of the momentum in that direction needed to reach the first minimum, $p_y = p \sin\theta$. So, $\Delta p_y \approx p \sin\theta$.
The total momentum of the electron is given by the de Broglie relation, $p = h/\lambda$.
Substituting these into the product $\Delta x \Delta p_y$:
$\Delta x \Delta p_y \approx a \times (p \sin\theta)$
Now, substitute the expressions for $p$ and $\sin\theta$:
$\Delta x \Delta p_y \approx a \times \left( \frac{h}{\lambda} \times \frac{\lambda}{a} \right)$
The terms $a$ and $\lambda$ cancel out, leaving:
$\Delta x \Delta p_y \approx h$.
This deduces a form of the Heisenberg uncertainty principle from the wave properties of matter.
